verses.text: On the third day Esther puts on her royal robes and stands in the court, and the king holds out the golden sceptre. The dreaded moment turns to favour. God, who holds the king's heart, can melt a frown to welcome in an instant.

On the third day Esther puts on her royal robes and stands in the court, and the king holds out the golden sceptre. The dreaded moment turns to favour. God, who holds the king's heart, can melt a frown to welcome in an instant.

Offered up to half the kingdom, Esther asks only that the king and Haman come to her banquet. She moves with patient wisdom, not haste. The cause of God is often best served by prayerful prudence that waits for the fitting moment.

Offered up to half the kingdom, Esther asks only that the king and Haman come to her banquet. She moves with patient wisdom, not haste. The cause of God is often best served by prayerful prudence that waits for the fitting moment.

Offered up to half the kingdom, Esther asks only that the king and Haman come to her banquet. She moves with patient wisdom, not haste. The cause of God is often best served by prayerful prudence that waits for the fitting moment.

At the banquet the king presses again, and Esther invites them to a second feast the next day. She delays her request once more. Wisdom knows that timing is everything, and trusts God to ripen the moment.

At the banquet the king presses again, and Esther invites them to a second feast the next day. She delays her request once more. Wisdom knows that timing is everything, and trusts God to ripen the moment.

At the banquet the king presses again, and Esther invites them to a second feast the next day. She delays her request once more. Wisdom knows that timing is everything, and trusts God to ripen the moment.

Haman leaves glad, but the sight of Mordecai unbowed fills him with rage, though he restrains himself till he reaches home. One small defiance poisons all his joy. The proud heart cannot rest while a single knee refuses it.

Haman leaves glad, but the sight of Mordecai unbowed fills him with rage, though he restrains himself till he reaches home. One small defiance poisons all his joy. The proud heart cannot rest while a single knee refuses it.

Haman boasts to his friends of his riches, his sons, his promotion, and the queen's private banquet. He counts his blessings as proof of his greatness. Pride feeds on its own glory, blind to how soon it can be stripped away.

Haman boasts to his friends of his riches, his sons, his promotion, and the queen's private banquet. He counts his blessings as proof of his greatness. Pride feeds on its own glory, blind to how soon it can be stripped away.

Yet, he says, all this means nothing while Mordecai sits at the gate; so his wife advises a gallows fifty cubits high, and he gladly builds it. Hatred robs him of every pleasure. The man who cannot enjoy his abundance for one ungranted grudge prepares his own downfall.
